data.title: Boosting After-Tax Returns: An 'All Of The Above' Effort
data.description:
By Paul Robertson | Gavin Romm, CFA | Mark Gleason, CFA

Better performance requires more than just focusing on pretax returns or minimizing tax expenses.

Improving after-tax returns is rarely as simple as boosting pretax returns or reducing tax expenses. It’s actually quite a bit more involved than that. As we see it, maximizing after-tax performance requires an “all of the above” approach, applying a range of techniques in a holistic way.

For investors, improving the path of returns via better up-down capture may drive better outcomes. Sources can be incorporating better building blocks, assembling them more efficiently, and adding alpha to enhance market returns. For investors with assets in taxable accounts, a sometimes-forgotten alpha source can be boosting after-tax returns—and that means reducing the tax bite.

On the surface of it, the math of after-tax return is simple. Take your returns before taxes, subtract the tax expenses you owe, and there you have it. But that seeming simplicity often leads people to narrow their focus to two variables: 1) boosting returns before taxes and 2) reducing tax expenses. But as always seems the case with taxes, it’s not that simple.

The After-Tax Return Equation Is More Complicated

There seems to be a never-ending stream of research into the ways investors can boost pretax returns, often by analyzing new data sets using new techniques, seeking to find patterns to unlock higher pretax returns. But those improvements aren’t a lock to flow through to the after-tax bottom line.

In fact, they might lead to steeper tax expenses—and the tax on short-term capital gains is a major culprit. Strategies designed to exploit high-frequency data sets are very likely to buy and sell securities quickly. That rapid turnover can create short-term capital gains taxed at a higher rate. The challenge with boosting pretax returns is to find a strategy that boosts returns enough to offset extra taxes.

The other variable in keeping more after taxes is to reduce tax expenses by taking advantage of opportunities in the tax code, and there’s an impressive inventory of tools to work with, spanning investment types, trading strategy and even investment vehicles.

Invest in municipal bonds. Muni bonds have a built-in tax advantage versus taxable bonds, such as corporate debt. Interest income from muni bonds isn’t subject to federal income taxes, and if the bonds are issued in an investor's home state, state taxes may not apply either. Taxable bonds, on the other hand, are subject to federal income taxes and most of them to state income taxes, too (Display).

Reduce portfolio turnover by holding investments longer. Less buying and selling reduce taxable transactions, and investments held for more than a year face a lower capital gains tax rate when sold. The top federal long-term capital gains tax rate is 20% (23.8% including the Medicare surtax); for short-term gains, it’s a whopping 37% (40.8% including the Medicare surtax). Likewise, qualified stock dividends (that meet a holding-period test) are taxed at the long-term capital gains rate; non-qualified dividends face a much higher tax rate—the same as short-term capital gains.

Tax Rates Make a Big Difference in Both Gains and Income

Representative Tax Rates for Capital Gains and Income (Percent)

Offset realized capital gains with realized capital losses. At times, investors need to sell securities, which could produce a realized capital gain. This prompts many investors to hurriedly scan their portfolios at the end of the year, looking for other holdings that are in the red and can be sold at a loss to reduce their yearly net capital gains tax expenses.

There’s the option of systematically slowing portfolio turnover and taking realized tax losses in a tax-management overlay strategy for strategies in separately managed accounts (SMAs). But implemented ineffectively, this could inhibit active management, ultimately leaving less pretax return to preserve.

Opt for tax-friendly vehicles. Tax-deferred accounts like individual retirement accounts or 401(k) accounts can protect assets from taxes, but their contribution limits require investors to reduce taxes on assets elsewhere. With partnerships or SMAs, net capital losses can be made available for investors to use immediately, offsetting realized gains from other securities. Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) offer tax advantages through their creation and redemption processes, which may defer capital gains for investors. Strategies likely to produce realized capital gains regularly might be better suited for an ETF; for those likely to generate no capital gains or to produce capital losses (such as direct-index tax-loss harvesting equity strategies and tax-managed munis), SMAs or partnerships could be the better fit.

Asking Questions to Size Up the Potential Tax Bite

If it seems like there are a lot of moving parts in tax management, there are. Improving after-tax returns is rarely as simple as increasing returns before taxes or paring back tax expenses. In our view, a holistic, “all of the above” approach makes sense. Investors with taxable assets should ask pointed questions when considering their options:

  • How does the investment strategy generate its return?
  • Does that approach result in high turnover (and possibly a higher tax profile)?
  • Will the taxable capital gains likely be mainly short term or long term?
  • Can the strategy accommodate a tax-management overlay?
  • Will that tax overlay have a significant impact on the strategy’s pretax returns?
  • Is the strategy available in a tax-efficient vehicle?

As we see it, taking home strong after-tax performance takes more than just focusing on pretax returns or minimizing tax expenses. A robust set of tax-management strategies, tailored to each investor's specific situation, may enable investors with taxable assets to keep more of what they earn.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: As Losses Endure, Babcock & Wilcox Has A Dilution And Cash Flow Problem (Downgrade)
data.description:
What a difference 18 months can make with a stock. Back in September of 2024, I initiated coverage on Babcock & Wilcox (BW) on the occasion of the company’s first quarter of earnings. EPS was only 1¢, and it wasn’t clear it was going to continue, so with uncertainty and cash flow weakness, I rated the formerly sub-$2 company a hold, advising investors to keep a close eye on the cash flow before making decisions. At present day, the stock is dramatically higher priced.

The stock surged late in 2025 and even further this week on news that they had come in ahead of estimates for Q4, and had received the go-ahead on a major new project, one that dwarfs the sort of backlog Babcock & Wilcox normally carries. The market cap is growing, but what about the company?

That’s a more complicated issue, and as we examine what’s happened from then to now, we need to revisit the struggling balance sheet, the cash flow issues, and some new potential problems on the horizon as the company tries to grow into a much bigger player with an AI data center electricity project.

Paying Down Debt, the Dilution Problem

The first new problem is dilution, and we’re going to start to look at that as we reexamine the company’s balance sheet. Back in 2024, Babcock & Wilcox was carrying a rather large amount of debt for a company their size and, conspicuously, also a deficit of equity, which is never a good sign. Today, things aren’t much better, and when you see how they managed to get “not much better,” you’ll see why dilution is a red flag.

Then

Now

Cash & Equivalents

$95 million

$89.5 million

Total Current Assets

$574.5 million

$470.7 million

Total Current Liabilities

$334 million

$386.7 million

Senior Notes

$339 million

$235 million

Total Shareholder Equity

($200 million)

($132 million)

Shares Outstanding

92.2 million

121.8 million

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

What happened here is pretty stark. The current ratio got somewhat worse, and we’ll see why that might matter in a bit, and there’s still a deficit of equity. Debt was down a bit, but it’s because some of the senior notes had to be paid off as they were coming due in Q4 of 2025. How did they pay it? They issued almost 30 million new shares of common stock, raising roughly $130 million.

You’ll notice also that the equity deficit shrank, but less than what it would’ve purely from a wildly dilutive issue of new shares. This is because the narrow profitability that Babcock & Wilcox enjoyed in Q3 of 2024 didn’t exactly last.

Return to Loss and the Cash Flow Problem

Babcock & Wilcox hasn’t been profitable on a GAAP basis, and while there were hopes that they’d reach non-GAAP profitability in 2025, as I mentioned in the last analysis, that didn’t happen. Rather, non-GAAP losses grew year-over-year.

2024

2025

Revenue

$581 million

$588 million

Operating Income

($6.4 million)

$20.8 million

Interest Expenses

($46.1 million)

($37.5 million)

Net Income

($74.8 million)

($51.0 million)

GAAP EPS

(82¢)

(48¢)

Non-GAAP EPS

(21¢)

(24¢)

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

From a GAAP perspective, things got slightly less bad here. Revenue didn’t meaningfully grow, but Babcock & Wilcox did manage to come up with something of an operating income, even if that was still quickly wiped out by the interest expenses they had to pay, and other non-operating expenses. In the end, the GAAP loss was smaller, but the non-GAAP loss was slightly bigger, which isn’t optimal.

Estimates for 2026 are hopeful that non-GAAP can get to slight profitability, with estimates of revenue of $723 million and EPS of 10¢, though I’d point out that back in September of 2024, the estimates at the time were that 2025 would enjoy revenue of $989 million and non-GAAP earnings of 13¢. As you can see, though they beat the estimates that were eventually offered after multiple downward revisions, Babcock & Wilcox was supposed to be non-GAAP profitable long ago, and they still aren’t there yet.

The operating income is an encouraging sign, taken on its own, but here again, the cash flow statement reveals it’s not as good as it seems.

2024

2025

Operating Cash Flow

($118.7 million)

($68.9 million)

Investing Cash Flow

$109.9 million

$197 million

Financing Cash Flow

$69.7 million

($58.7 million)

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

Once again, operations improved, but there’s still a lot of work left to do, as Babcock & Wilcox still didn’t manage to muster operating cash flow profitability. Two other problems stand out to me, though.

The first is that the only meaningful positive cash flow Babcock & Wilcox made in 2025 was from the sale of assets and parts of the business that they considered “unprofitable.” This is obviously unsustainable, because Babcock & Wilcox has more liabilities than assets, hence the negative equity on the balance sheet. Moreover, since the company in general isn’t operating profitably, arguably anything could fairly be called an “unprofitable” business segment.

The second is that financing cash flow ended up negative in 2025. Again, remember that section included a positive $130 million from issuing new shares of stock, and all that dilution was still dragged down to negative cash flow. That included paying down the debt somewhat, but the absence of operating profitability, I think, portends further needs to raise capital, and the reasons for that will become apparent in the next section.

The Base Electron Project: AI Data Center Power

The recent surge in stock price for Babcock & Wilcox comes with the announcement, concurrent with their Q4 earnings statement, that they’ve received a notice to proceed on a major project they’re doing with Base Electron. That project is valued at $2.4 billion, and involves four natural gas-fired boilers and turbines to provide electricity to major AI data center campuses belonging to Applied Digital (APLD).

It’s a massive project from Babcock & Wilcox’s perspective, as the company hasn’t normally been involved in projects numbered in the billions of dollars. Given how excited the market is about AI data centers these days, it should also come as no surprise that the market reacted very positively to the announcement.

That said, it remains an open question how much this will actually add to the bottom line. We don’t have a track record of Babcock & Wilcox doing projects anywhere near this size where we can reasonably guess the margin they might be able to realize on them. Similarly, since the margin on the projects they have been doing doesn’t boil down to a profit, it’s fair to ask whether this will make them serious money at all.

On top of that, the relative financial weakness of Babcock & Wilcox could be an issue here as well. This company is not structured to handle capital-intensive projects this large, because they never have before. If they need to raise more capital in the course of funding a project of this scope, that’s likely to be substantially further dilutive in the near term. The hope here is that this huge project will lead to other huge projects and that the huge projects will prove more profitable than the small projects historically have been. In the end, either is possible, but neither is guaranteed.

A Possible Route to Profitability but Backlog Problems

While I would say the underlying “risk” of my relatively bearish thesis is that Babcock & Wilcox could absolutely knock this project out of the park, secure a strong margin on it, and attract further substantial projects, right now the backlog shows another potential problem.

Backlog reported at year’s end

2023

$368.2 million

2024

$540.1 million

2025

$2.8 billion

(Source: 10-K from 2025 and press release from Q4 earnings)

The backlog growth from 2023 to 2024 was healthy growth in orders, and while Babcock & Wilcox didn’t end up turning a profit in the following fiscal year, it showed at the very least that there was healthy demand for the services they offer. The numbers at the end of 2025 suggest the opposite.

While the 2025 numbers are much larger, it’s entirely because the backlog includes the aforementioned $2.4 billion project. But for that project, the backlog actually got substantially weaker in 2025, and would only be in the realm of $400 million otherwise. This is a major concentration risk.

Even if the big project goes rather well from a margin perspective, it is at the moment the overwhelming majority of the backlog, so the company’s ability to gain future projects of that size is going to be an important indicator of how transformative the project will actually be.

Too Many Questions

When asking about how to handle my prior hold rating, I ask myself whether Babcock & Wilcox is currently worth the numbers it is trading at. Between the lack of equity, the lack of earnings, enormous dilution, and uncertainty about how well they will handle such an uncharacteristically large project, I feel the answer is no.

That’s why, for now, I’m downgrading Babcock & Wilcox to a strong sell, because for the company to justify its current level, a lot of things need to go not just right, but transformationally perfect. For a company not used to tackling projects on this scale, some hurdles seem virtually inevitable, and aren’t being priced in here.

AI data centers may be an exciting industry that gets the market talking, but the visibility on Babcock & Wilcox going through a project related to that to become a massively larger and more successful company than they are right now just isn’t there. The company might successfully navigate this opportunity into a successful venture, but the high price we’re already asked to pay suggests the market is getting far ahead of itself.

Investors should keep an eye on not just how the project is going, but how the backlog is faring in future quarters. Right now the company has a relative paucity of other things to work on after this big project, and that’s not the way this company will grow.

data.title: Hyperscalers In The Backseat, Memory And Gas Turbines To The Front
data.description:
By Xavier Stonehouse

The current landscape of AI infrastructure has reached a critical inflection point where the dominant hyperscalers are being forced into the backseat by the physical and economic realities of the hardware they require. As DRAM contract prices skyrocket - with DDR5 projected to jump 62% quarter-over-quarter in early 2026 and nearly 400% year-over-year - the total cost of ownership (TCO) for datacenters is undergoing a radical shift. Memory now accounts for up to 40% of total rack costs, driving a scenario where the four major hyperscalers are forecast to spend a combined $650 billion in 2026 alone. This "existential spend" is no longer just about the fastest chips; it is a desperate race to secure scarce memory, silicon, and the power required to actually turn them on.

The "Big Three" memory manufacturers - Samsung (SSNLF), SK Hynix, and Micron (MU) - play a critical role as the industry transitions to High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). The market's recognition of this power shift is evident in the stock performance over the past 12 months, with all three companies experiencing significant appreciation. To navigate this shortage, some companies are entering aggressive Long-Term Agreements (LTAs), essentially buying future chips before the factories are even built. Micron announced that its entire 2026 high‑bandwidth memory output, including HBM4, is already sold out under long-term contracts. SK Hynix as well reported that its DRAM, NAND, and HBM production capacity is essentially sold out through this year. This massive industrialization effort has also sent the values of Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) providers higher as they race to build out multi-billion dollar "greenfield" projects like SK Hynix’s Yongin facility and Micron’s new Idaho and Tongluo fabs.

However, all of this silicon procurement is rapidly being overshadowed by a looming power wall. Speaking on the Dwarkesh Patel podcast, Elon Musk explicitly defined this bottleneck: "The current limiting factor that I see in the three to four year timeframe is chips. In the one-year timeframe, it’s energy, power production, and electricity". Heavy-duty gas turbines, the primary solution for data center power, have a 3-4 year lead time and are essentially sold out through 2028 and into 2030. Producing more of them quickly is incredibly difficult because the limiting factor is casting the specialized turbine vanes and blades - a highly complex process handled by only three massively backlogged casting companies in the world.

Because hyperscalers are aggressively buying up next-generation chips like Nvidia's (NVDA) Blackwell, they are entering a precarious financial phase where these new clusters are used exclusively for training new models, which generates no immediate ROI. Currently, hyperscalers generate most of their return on invested capital (ROIC) by applying GPUs to make their core recommender systems and advertising algorithms more efficient. However, we are already seeing their ROIC plateau and aggressively decline - with Meta (META) falling from 31.9% to 21.9% and Amazon (AMZN) dropping from 14.8% to 12.3% - because the sheer magnitude of upfront infrastructure costs is overwhelming their near-term revenue generation. The market is actively demonstrating its anxiety over this dynamic through recent stock price drops; Big Tech has erased more than $1.3 trillion in combined market value in early 2026, with Microsoft (MSFT) shares falling nearly 16% year-to-date as AI spending faces intense Wall Street scrutiny.

Memory Prices Rising Quickly

To summarize the timeline of these bottlenecks as we see it: we are currently in early 2026, hitting the severe memory and SSD wall while hyperscalers sign LTAs locking themselves into peak pricing. Between 2026 and 2027, hyperscalers will likely suffer the predicted drag in ROIC (the "air gap") as massive capex is deployed for training without immediate revenue offset. By late 2026, the energy wall may truly hit as the physical delivery of chips begins to exceed the grid's ability to turn them on. Relief from the memory wall will likely not arrive until late 2027 or 2028, when major new "greenfield" fabs finally begin producing meaningful wafer output. Finally, the energy wall may be definitively solved around 2028 to 2029 (roughly 36 months out) by bypassing the terrestrial grid and launching AI data centers into space.

Taken together, these dynamics may increase the strategic importance of memory manufacturers and certain infrastructure suppliers within the broader AI ecosystem. As hyperscalers moderate capital expenditures following significant investment cycles, market leadership could broaden beyond the largest technology platforms. This environment has the potential to create opportunities in segments of the market tied to physical infrastructure and energy capacity - areas that have historically received less investor attention relative to software-oriented businesses.

References:

  • Ball, Jamin, and David Flynn. "Clouded Judgement 2.20.26 - The SSD / Memory Reckoning." Clouded Judgement, 20 Feb. 2026.
  • Huang, Jensen. Keynote Address. Consumer Electronics Show (CES), NVIDIA, Jan. 2026.
  • "Hyperscaler Research 2026-2030 Outlook." Technology Dashboard, 20 Feb. 2026.
  • Musk, Elon. Interview by Nikhil Kamath. People by WTF, Episode 16, 2026. ---. "In 36 months, the cheapest place to put AI will be space." 2026.
  • Patel, Dylan, et al. "Memory Mania: How a Once-in-Four-Decades Shortage Is Fueling a Memory Boom." SemiAnalysis, 6 Feb. 2026.
  • SemiAnalysis. "InferenceX v2: NVIDIA Blackwell Vs AMD vs Hopper - Formerly InferenceMAX." Feb. 2026.
  • "The 2026 Semiconductor Supercycle: Memory Pricing and WFE Dynamics." Market Intelligence Report, 19 Feb. 2026.
  • "The Memory Bottleneck and the Semi Cap Cycle." Industry Analysis, Feb. 2026.
  • Wong, Gerald, and Dylan Patel. "CPUs are Back: The Datacenter CPU Landscape in 2026." SemiAnalysis, 9 Feb. 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: India To Buy Russian Oil As War Disrupts Supplies
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

U.S. issues 30 day waiver (1:00) and considers requiring permits for global AI chip sales (2:45) China approves Pfizer’s GLP-1 (4:00)

Transcript

U.S. stocks ended lower on Thursday as investors assessed renewed geopolitical tensions in the U.S.-Israel vs. Iran conflict that led to oil price surges.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed -0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) ended -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) finished -1.6%.

U.S. Treasury yields, on the other hand, moved higher across the curve, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rising 4 basis points to 4.14% and the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) gaining 3 basis points to 3.59%.

The United States on Thursday issued a 30-day waiver to India for purchasing crude from Moscow as the Iran war upends global supplies. The waiver on purchasing Russian oil is expected to help ease global supply worries, with India being the world’s fourth-biggest refiner and fifth-largest exporter of petroleum products.

A license issued late on Thursday covers transactions related to Russian crude oil and petroleum products loaded onto vessels before March 5, so long as they're delivered to India and purchased by an Indian firm. The measure expires April 4 at 12:01 a.m. Washington time.

India faces risks from energy supply disruptions, with crude oil reserves sufficient for only about 25 days of demand. Around 40% of the country’s oil imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz from the Middle East.

Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI crude (CL1:COM) recouped some losses after falling over 1% on Friday and were last trading at $85.19 and $80.60 per barrel, respectively.

This “short-term measure will not provide significant financial benefit” to Russia as it only allows transactions of oil already stranded at sea, U.S. Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessent said in a post on X.

The U.S. government, meanwhile, is taking steps to curb rising oil prices, including offering political risk insurance for tankers transiting the Gulf. U.S. crude prices have climbed about 20% this week on the back of the escalating conflict in the Middle East.

Officials at the US Commerce Department have reportedly written draft regulations that would restrict AI chip shipments to anywhere in the world without American approval, giving Washington broad control over whether other countries can build facilities for training and running artificial-intelligence models, and under what conditions.

The proposed regulations would require companies to seek U.S. permission for virtually all exports of AI accelerators from the likes of Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), a global expansion of curbs that currently cover around 40 countries, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

These chips are the most coveted components in the tech world. Companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) buy them by the thousands to install in data centers that run services like ChatGPT and Gemini.

Shares of Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) fell to session lows on the news Thursday. Nvidia dropped as much as 1.9%, while AMD declined 2.3%.

President Donald Trump’s team has said repeatedly that they want the world to use American AI, and the draft rules aren’t meant to function as an Nvidia (NVDA) export ban. Rather, the regulation would set up the U.S. government as gatekeeper for the AI industry: Companies — and in some cases, their governments — would have to seek the blessing of the U.S. Commerce Department to buy the precious accelerators, the report added.

China has approved a new obesity drug from Pfizer (PFE), intensifying competition in a market that is expected to grow even more crowded with upcoming generic versions.

The drug, ecnoglutide, is approved for chronic weight management in overweight or obese adults, Pfizer (PFE) announced on Friday via WeChat, according to reports. The company secured China rights to the therapy from local startup Hangzhou Sciwind Bioscience in a $495 million deal in late February. Ecnoglutide ​is also ​approved in China as a treatment for Type II diabetes.

The injection belongs to the class of GLP-1 receptor agonist drugs sold locally by drugmakers such as Novo Nordisk (NVO), ‌Eli Lilly (LLY), and ⁠Innovent Biologics (IVBIY).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery And Paramount Skydance: A Lower-Risk Arb Play And A Leveraged Bet
data.description:
We Have A Winner

Nearly three months have passed since I covered the competing bids for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) by Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY). Both potential buyers maintained fiscal discipline and avoided a heated bidding war. Netflix made no changes to its original bid, except to change it to an all-cash deal of $27.75 per share, a move that may have been necessitated by the drop in NFLX shares below the lower collar limit of the deal. Paramount raised their bid just $1 to $31, plus $0.25 per quarter for each quarter after Sep. 30 if the deal is not closed. Paramount also made stronger commitments to obtain equity and debt financing to close the deal and refinance outstanding WBD debt. Finally, Paramount has already paid the $2.8 billion breakup fee to Netflix and committed to a $7 billion fee payable to WBD if their deal does not close due to regulatory reasons. Unchanged from the original bid was Paramount's deal to buy the whole company, rather than spinning off the cable and broadcast assets into a new company, as would have been necessary under the Netflix deal. This reinforced the superiority of the Paramount deal, as we have seen a similar spinoff, Versant (VSNT), trade down since its spinoff from Comcast (CMCSA). WBD's board found the new offer to be superior, and Netflix walked away rather than bid higher.

After some initial excitement and speculation of higher bids causing WBD shares to trade around $30, they settled back down and are now just slightly above their early December levels. Paramount Skydance has traded lower, probably on the prospects of share dilution and added debt, with a bump once they were declared the winner. Netflix shares headed lower from the point of their offer but reversed quickly when they walked away and pocketed $2.8 billion.

The combined company will be a strong but not anticompetitive player in streaming. It will also have valuable news and sports franchises to help the linear networks be viable for longer. Finally, it will be a content powerhouse with two studios producing well-known movie and TV franchises along with a huge library dating back 100 years.

WBD now looks like a relatively low-risk arb play for a quick 11% gain through anticipated close in September. Paramount Skydance is still a riskier bet with dilution of existing shareholders and added leverage. It could turn out fine, but it depends on achieving an ambitious $6 billion in merger synergies.

WBD Perspective: Easy To Understand Payout, Pretty Good Odds

For WBD shareholders, the expected outcome is simple. Hold until the deal closes, probably by the end of Q3 2026, and receive $31 in cash for each WBD share. Any delay beyond Q3 results in an additional payout of $0.25 per quarter at close. There is no longer any need to submit shares into a tender offer. Shareholders only need to vote to approve the deal at a special meeting to be held in the next couple of months.

The chances of regulators blocking the deal appear low. From an anticompetition lens, the merger creates a streaming service with about 22% market share in the US, similar to Netflix and Amazon (AMZN) Prime Video. That is considerably below the 34% share that a combined Netflix-HBO Max would have had.

On the network side, the deal combines the Turner networks (TBS, TNT, CNN) and Discovery from the WBD side with MTV, Nickelodeon, and BET from the Paramount side, as well as some smaller channels. This is a decent chunk of the cable lineup, but would still have major competitors, including Versant, Comcast, and Disney (DIS). Looking specifically at broadcast networks, which regulators often scrutinize more closely, the combined company would still have only CBS in the US. WBD has some broadcast networks internationally. From the perspective of news, CBS and CNN will continue to operate independently.

On the studio side, both Warner Bros. and Paramount studios will continue to operate independently and will each produce about 15 movies per year with dedicated run time in theaters before moving to paid video on demand and streaming.

At the federal level in the US, there appear to be few regulatory obstacles to close. Management stated on the merger conference call that "the waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act has expired, and there are no statutory impediments to close in the United States." The Federal Communications Commission has also signaled it is unlikely to block the deal. At the state level, especially in California, officials have promised to examine the deal closely and could file suit to try to block the deal if they are not satisfied the company will follow through on its commitment to keep both studios running and minimize the impact on the entertainment industry. Internationally, Paramount notes that Germany and Slovenia have already approved the deal, and the company will continue to work with other countries in the coming months.

If the deal does not close, WBD gets the $7 billion breakup fee from Paramount ($2.69/share). There is also the chance that Netflix or someone else could make another bid for the company, but probably lower than the previous $27.75 offer. So, worst case, I do not see WBD shares heading back to the $12 they were trading at before any merger talk started.

Combined Company Profile

The merged company will also merge the streaming services (predominantly HBO Max and Paramount+, but also Discovery+ and Pluto TV) into one service. The combined service will have a century of content from the two studios, popular TV series, both native to streaming and brought over from the broadcast and cable networks, sports, including the NFL, UFC, and Olympics (outside the US), and news (CBS and CNN). This is a much deeper and broader content base than Netflix. It is starting out with an already global-scale subscriber base of around 200 million across 100 countries.

While the network business continues to fight the headwinds of cord cutting and short attention spans, the combined linear portfolio provides opportunities for cost cutting as well as a bigger content base to draw more advertisers. This content can also be packaged for streaming as that business grows.

On the studio side, the company will continue operating both studios and producing about 30 movies per year as well as TV content and third-party productions. The WBD side brings franchises like DC Comics, Harry Potter, and Game of Thrones, while the Paramount side includes Star Trek, Top Gun, and Yellowstone, along with many other Taylor Sheridan-produced series.

Financially, the combined company will have about $69 billion in revenue and $12 billion in EBITDA, before an additional $6 billion in expected synergies. The synergies are expected to come from 5 different sources. Two deal with technology, and three deal with corporate rationalizations and economies of scale.

The tech savings, from combining the streaming services and ERP systems, are reasonable expectations but come with the risk of delays and poor project execution. If the combined streaming app does not work well from the start, it could cause customer churn or limit new subscriptions. On the corporate side, eliminating redundant corporate jobs and offices looks achievable but is going to be limited by the commitment to keep both studios and two news services in CBS and CNN. Savings in marketing and advertising are also harder to achieve when keeping two studios with their own branding.

Deal Math

Paramount is paying $31 for WBD's 2.6 million shares, or a total of 80.9 billion. Paramount is also funding the $2.8 billion breakup fee to Netflix (already spent) and has committed to keeping $5 billion in cash on the balance sheet of the combined company. The Ellison family and RedBird are contributing $47 billion and will receive equity in the form of new PSKY shares at a share price of $16.02. Existing Paramount shareholders are expected to be able to participate in a rights offering to buy PSKY shares at this price as well; however, this funding is not included in this calculation and is out of the money at the current market price anyway. The rest of the cash for the deal comes from $36.8 billion of new debt issued by Paramount.

The deal takes the share count of PSKY from around 1.13 billion shares currently to 4.06 billion after the deal and increases net debt from $15.8 billion to $82.7 billion, including the assumed debt from WBD.

Valuation

The combined company is expected to have pro forma 2026 EBITDA of $12.1 billion, or $18.1 billion if all $6 billion of synergies are achieved. Based on a combined enterprise value of $147.8 billion, the EV/sales will be 2.2 and the EV/EBITDA will be 12.2 (no synergies) or 8.2 (full synergies). This is in line with the valuation of Disney, but far below that of Netflix. This could be because the expected 2026-2030 sales growth of the combined company is "mid-single digits", close to the 3.6% for Disney from the Seeking Alpha Earnings page, but barely half the expected growth for Netflix. I should note that "mid-single digit" sales growth for the combined company is more optimistic than the analyst consensus of just 1.3% for existing WBD and 0.1% for PSKY.

The combined company is starting out with high debt levels. The net debt/EBITDA leverage is 6.8 using 2026 numbers without any synergies. Including the $6 billion of synergies, leverage would be about 4.6, and the company expects to get to a leverage ratio of 3 within 3 years of closing based on achieving the mid-single-digit sales growth and EBITDA margins in the mid-20s % range.

Conclusion

Based on the progress already made with regulators, WBD looks like a lower-risk arb play to earn about 11% total between now and the merger close expected in September (or double that on an annualized basis). Risks include rejection by a foreign regulator or challenges from state attorneys general, but I expect in those cases, Paramount could work out some divestment deal or hiring commitment to allow the deal to close. Any delay in that scenario would yield another $0.25 per quarter for WBD shareholders.

For the merged PSKY-WBD, the $6 billion of synergies expected looks ambitious, particularly with the commitment to keep both studios and both news organizations intact. However, even with about half the proposed synergies, the combined company would be valued similarly to Disney based on sales and EBITDA. Leverage would still be higher, however, even by the end of 2029 with full synergies achieved. Given the uncertainty of forecasted synergies and higher leverage, PSKY remains a hold.

As far as Netflix, the time to buy it was when rumors arose of PSKY's new bid being considered superior by the WBD board. After the 20%+ gain since then, it once again looks expensive, even if you believe the 9.7% sales growth projections compared to mid-single digits for the merged PSKY.

data.title: The Williams Companies: High Valuation And Technical Caution May Not Fuel Its Upside
data.description:
A few months after I rated The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB) a buy stock, its value has already increased by approximately 33%. This is supported by its robust Q4 2025 performance and sound fundamentals. But at this level, I believe that WMB has already had a good run, and entering might not be as attractive as before. External considerations amid geopolitical tensions may also have some impact or spillovers on its performance. Technicals adhere to it, as overbuying warrants caution despite sustained bullish momentum.

WMB Q4 2025: Its Fueled Strength In Uncertain Times

The oil and gas market continues to experience volatility due to energy commodity price swings, stubborn inflation, tariff woes, and geopolitical tensions. Even an established player with a heavy concentration on natural gas is not entirely shielded from potential market headwinds. The Williams Companies, Inc. is also susceptible to these risks. A small change in prices can easily affect its performance. Even so, its resilient business model, backed by its prudent expansion and strategic geographical diversification, allows it to ensure operational stability while pushing revenue growth. We have all seen this in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $3.19B ($11.95B - $8.75B), up by 16.7% YoY from $2.74B ($10.50B - $7.76B). Note that it’s reported as $3.108B on Seeking Alpha, as it excluded the net gain/(loss) from commodity derivatives. Anyway, its solid revenue increase was mainly driven by its service revenues, which comprised 70% of the total revenue. They have a combined amount of $2.23B, which was 10.2% higher than in Q4 2024 at only $2.02B. This can be attributed to its expansion projections and higher volumes supported by acquisitions. Likewise, its product sales increased, which served as its comeback after the negative YoY sales growth in Q3 2025. We can attribute it to the sharp rebound of the natural gas price. During the quarter, the price reached its three-year high after exceeding $5.00. So, the value of natural gas processed and transported also increased. Overall, WMB still enjoyed double-digit revenue growth.

If we look at its performance per segment. Its operations in the Gathering, Processing, and Crude Oil Transportation remained the backbone of its growth. Once again, volumes had a notable increase on a YoY and QoQ basis. NGL production has also grown uninterruptedly for eight consecutive quarters. This was quite impressive considering the weaker oil price in Q4 due to the OPEC oil hike. The strong natural gas price trend also helped it since it became more profitable for producers. With increased production, WMB was able to process and transport more volumes. It was also able to set a more favorable rate. Its sustained increase in capex allowed it to expand its operating capacity across various segments and locations. As a result, natural gas transportation and storage revenues rose by 10.3%. Meanwhile, its gathering and processing, or G&P revenues in the Gulf of America rose by $88M, or 51%, YoY. This was its strongest growth or the whole 2025.

More interestingly, WMB was able to reduce its operating costs and expenses, which shows improved efficiency despite its larger operating capacity. It also benefited from lower oil prices because it lowered fuel prices. WMB uses it to operate gas processing plants, gathering systems, and pipeline compressor stations. As a result, its operating margin remained high at over 41% versus 29% YoY.

Key Considerations

As a midstream company, the Middle East tension may not directly and immediately impact its revenues. After all, it operates as a midstream company. It doesn’t have pipelines or production fields in the region. Also, it doesn’t ship through the Strait of Hormuz. However, there may be some spillovers into its business, and I think the net effect is positive theoretically.

On the positive side, the tension may sustain the oil price uptrend. Today, Brent and WTI are at their 13-month highs of $84.04 and $76.96 per barrel. And I’m sure you know that higher oil prices make crude oil exploration and production more enticing and profitable. With that, there may be more US drilling activity, which may lead to more oil and gas flowing through WMB’s pipelines. In the end, pipeline and/or G&P revenues may increase.

Of course, this is not yet guaranteed because we also have to understand some nuances. Higher oil prices make refined oil products like fuel more expensive. Because it also uses fuel, it may also incur higher expenses. But because of its location, I think this downside risk is limited.

Drivers

One thing I like about it is its midstream pipeline transportation business model. This means that revenues primarily come from contracts and fees. So, it has limited risk exposure to oil and price fluctuations. Second, its cash flow is affected by oil and gas volumes more than prices. So even if their prices move sharply because of the current tension, pipeline revenue may remain stable. It may also capitalize on its location. Why? The Middle East tension may disrupt Middle East energy supply, so global consumers may shift to other sources or exporters like the US.

Another core strength is its robust liquidity. Because its operations remain profitable, it also enjoys high operating cash inflow to cover capex. For the whole year, its FCF reached $1.0B. WMB complements it with prudent debt management through time issuance and repayments. This helps it cover other outflows like dividends and share buybacks.

Considering this, its cash and cash equivalents remain ample at over $60M. It also has $2.1B in accounts receivable, which serve as its collectible revenues. Their combined amount is more than enough to cover borrowings maturing within one year. Meanwhile, the maturity profile of its borrowings is still decent since 95% of the total will not mature within a year. If we look at its maturity table, more than 70% of the total amount has a maturity of five years and above. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of 4.2x, which means that WMB needs 4.2 years to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it still has a huge buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

If we look at external factors, I believe it will benefit from the AI and digital revolution as the primary driver of global data center expansion. From 2025 to 2030, US power demand from data centers is expected to more than double. Because natural gas remains the main electricity source, companies involved in it will benefit. WMB is positioned for it due to its natural gas focused operations and strategies.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $2.10 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.525. Its growth rate is 4.56%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 5.5% to be in line with my previous coverage. This is still quite conservative since it is higher than the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, my estimated Beta is 0.65, which is a bit lower than 0.69 in my previous coverage. This means that the stock became less volatile than in my previous coverage. It is also less volatile than the S&P 500. With all this information, I derived a TP or target price, of $62.91. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $56.62. My new TP justifies the price rebound of WMB. However, it has already increased more than necessary, making it challenging to enter at this level.

Technicals

WMB’s technicals are still bullish as the price uptrend remains pronounced. It is also trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is trending upwards, which tells us that recent market sentiments have been generally optimistic.

However, MACD and RSI signal some caution. As you can see, the MACD line appears to be shifting downwards and is about to cross below the signal line, which suggests decreasing buying volume and increasing selling pressure. In addition, the histogram is still within positive territory but almost zero or neutral, which implies that buying volume is still higher than selling volume. But the latter has already caught up with the former. The gap between buying and selling volume has already narrowed. This also shows that investors are starting to see that WMB is already overpriced. Its RSI adheres to it because the stock is oversold.

Conclusion

The Williams Companies, Inc. remains promising with its robust performance and improved efficiency amid its increasing capacity. This is supported by its solid liquidity, which positions it well for potential opportunities. However, valuation is already stretched with some downside risks. Technicals also warrant some caution due to overbuying and increasing selling volume. I’m downgrading my rating from buy to hold for now.

data.title: Rates: Lessons From The Iraq War
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Can we identify echos from the 2003 Iraq war for Treasuries and rates?

In 2003, in the weeks leading up to the war in Iraq, the US 10yr Treasury yield fell from 3.95% to 3.55%, a 40bp drop over a three-to-four-week period. Not all of this was reflective of the upcoming war, but a lot of it was. There was an overt build-up of military presence as the coalition of the willing got ready. Hostilities finally kicked off on 20 March 2003, by which time the 10yr yield had popped back up to the 4.1% area. And in the first couple of weeks of the attack on Iran, the 10yr yield fell back down to the 3.8% area. The dominant impact impulse, over consecutive weeks, was in the direction of lower yields (a flight into Treasuries).

Just for context, the Fed funds rate was at 1.25% at the time (tail end of the dot.com bust). In fact, it got cut to a 1% low later in 2003. While the funds rate is at a different level today, it just so happens that the 10yr yield is in the same ballpark as it was back then. The events of the past few days are clearly not a perfect repeat of the Iraq War. But it is interesting to view the magnitude of change. We're not suggesting this is determinative; just making the comparison to help set some expectations and identify some differences. One was a much lower effect on the oil price back then (Iraqi exports had already been slashed, so the oil price impact was minimal).

Fast-forwarding to today, we identify some important nuances

Turning to now, the US 10yr yield shot to below 4% (to just above 3.9%) as the war with Iran broke out but quickly reverted to above 4% (hitting 4.1% briefly). At the extreme, that's a 20bp swing. It's also a far swifter reversion higher in yield than would be expected based on the Iraq War experience. The 2/10yr curve has also flattened, as it did in the lead-up to the Iraq War. This flattening process is quite striking and fits with the notion that a flight-to-safety trade continues to exist for Treasuries. This coincides with quite benign inflation expectations in the 10yr, with the implied break-even inflation rate there not deviating too far from 2.3% (absolutely fine).

But it's on the shorter tenors where we find spikes in inflation expectations. The 2yr break-even was, in fact, already elevated on the eve of last weekend's attacks, at around 2.8%. Now it's at 2.9%, reflective of the material rise in the price of oil. Beyond that, real yields have not done much, so the rise in break-evens is purely a function of higher regular yields. This tells us that markets are not, so far, discounting a material US economic activity risk from the current situation. Rather, the biggest risk is upward pressure on consumer prices. This, in turn, is anticipated to be temporary (long-term inflation break-evens remain tolerably benign).

Impact on European rates and projections for the second quarter

In terms of spreads to eurozone rates and yields, there is no big story to be told. The issue for Europe is its greater reliance on imported oil and acceptance of whatever the global price for energy is. Not to say that we do not see the same for the US. Rather, for Europe, it's more mechanical, albeit muted by a higher taxation element in the overall price set for energy. There has been quite a high correlation between eurozone and US rates since the conflict began, and we expect to see more of the same ahead.

We risk seeing increased bouts of flight to safety, pushing yields down. Don't rule out a break back below 4% on the US 10yr, even if brief, likely on a risk-off episode should things turn really sour. This could see the 10yr Bund yield falling back towards 2.5%. But as we look into the second quarter, we anticipate the US 10yr yield to get back up to the 4.3% area (a level that we observed for a period in January). That equates to the German 10yr yield rising to the 2.9% area. This reflects a resultant higher inflation narrative (higher energy prices). Beyond that, a subsequent calming in yields through the second half of the year would reflect the payment for that in terms of a hit to real growth.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Whirlpool: Investors Could Clean Up Buying At 52-Week Lows
data.description:
In October 2025, I wrote a bullish article about Whirlpool (WHR) when the stock traded in the $71 range. Whirlpool shares rallied to around $92 in February, but the stock has declined significantly since then. I have been buying this stock on sharp pullbacks for the past couple of years and selling after big gains. I think it is time to buy again. A lot has happened with Whirlpool since my last article, including Q4 earnings results and a recent capital raise that surprised many investors, sending the stock down to new 52-week lows. With this in mind, let's take a closer look at what might be yet another buying opportunity:

The Chart

As shown below, this stock traded for about $92 per share in February, but it has since plunged and recently hit new 52-week lows, just below $60 per share. This stock is now trading way below the 50-day moving average, which is around $79, and the 200-day moving average, which is just over $81. The only positive I see for the bulls on this chart is that Whirlpool shares now appear deeply oversold. This suggests a counter-trend rally could be coming soon.

I wanted to include this longer-term chart of Whirlpool shares because it shows the stock is now trading around the $60 lows it reached (and held) during the Covid pandemic market plunge. After hitting this low in 2020, the stock rebounded very sharply. This suggests the stock might be at strong support levels now, and if history repeats, this stock could be poised to rebound from current levels.

A Surprising Secondary Share Offering Creates Management Credibility Issues

In late February, Whirlpool shocked many investors when it announced it was raising capital through a secondary share offering. The company sold shares at $69 per share and raised nearly $1 billion, in an effort to reduce leverage. Since the management team cut the dividend in 2025, I think many investors believed that management had done this to free up cash flow to pay down debt, and that this action would be sufficient. So, it was surprising to see management make yet another painful move against shareholders with this secondary offering.

Many shareholders are likely fed up with the piecemeal approach to improving the balance sheet, leaving some of us wondering what the next shoe to drop from management is. Many investors did not think that Whirlpool needed to raise capital at this time, and I agree. But, even if they did need to raise capital, it could have been timed better, when the stock was trading at much higher levels. In management's defense, it is possible that they were concerned with a credit rating downgrade, which would raise borrowing costs, and perhaps they felt they had no choice but to raise capital to improve the balance sheet.

In my last article, I pointed out that David Tepper, a hedge fund billionaire, had taken a large stake in Whirlpool. Mr. Tepper weighed in on the decision to raise capital, calling it a "striking lack of judgment." He suggested that the cost of the offering was done at more than 10%, even as Whirlpool's debt trades at a tax-adjusted cost of capital below 5%. This indicates that the capital raise was not just poorly timed, but also potentially unwise. I think all of this creates a situation where many investors might view Whirlpool as lacking in credibility. Mr. Tepper sent a letter to Whirlpool after the share offering, and he said:

“Over the years this management team has destroyed hundreds of millions of dollars of shareholder value. Enough is enough. There can be no more excuses.”

Q4 Results

On January 28, 2026, Whirlpool announced Q4 results. Non-GAAP earnings of $1.10 per share missed by $0.42, and revenues came in at $4.1 billion, which was down 0.7% year-over-year. This also missed consensus estimates by $170 million. The company also guided for full-year GAAP earnings per diluted share of roughly $6.25 and ongoing earnings per share of around $7. In the Q4 press release, Marc Bitzer, Chairman and CEO, made comments that seem to suggest better times were ahead, which is another reason why many shareholders may have been blindsided by the announcement of a capital raise that was announced just about 1 month later. This is one reason why some shareholders might feel Whirlpool management has a credibility issue. Marc Bitzer stated:

"With a challenging 2025 behind us, our confidence for 2026 is based on our recent successful product launches, reduced promotional intensity and a gradual recovery of the housing market."

Short Interest

According to shortsqueeze.com, nearly 9.8 million shares are currently short, and this represents over 6 days worth of daily trading volume. Short sellers have done well lately, and many have made quick gains when you consider this stock was trading for about $92 per share just last month and now it is trading for just around $60 per share. While the capital raise was not taken well by the market, the stock is now oversold, and I think taking this stock down to just below $60 recently, is probably an overreaction. When this stock shows clear signs of having hit rock bottom, the short sellers could decide to cover, and that might help fuel a short-covering rally in the stock.

Here Are Some Positives And Potential Upside Catalysts

1) Activist Shareholders Could Push For A New CEO: I feel management has credibility issues since they cut the dividend last year and then raised capital in February after making what seemed to be positive comments about 2026 in the Q4 results. I don't think management helped their credibility by cutting the dividend significantly, only to follow it up with a capital raise a few months later. I believe any balance sheet leverage concerns should have been dealt with thoroughly and at one time.

I also think that the timing of the capital raise was not optimal since the stock was at higher levels even recently. With the recent letter that Mr. Tepper sent to Whirlpool, he might be looking for the Board of Directors to make management changes. At this point, it would not surprise me if the CEO were replaced, and I think investors would welcome that decision and possibly bid up the stock if that happened.

2) Whirlpool Is An AI Beneficiary: It seems like many stocks and even entire sectors have been hit hard daily due to concerns about AI disruption. I believe investors are starting to pay more attention to companies that have business models and products that cannot be disrupted by AI. It seems clear that human beings will always need appliances to clean clothes, and refrigerate and cook food, and this is why investors can eliminate this risk of AI disruption when it comes to Whirlpool. The other positive is that Whirlpool appears to be an AI beneficiary, since it could use AI to reduce costs in many ways, including reducing staff.

3) The Housing Market Could Improve: The housing market has been frozen for years due to high-interest rates. However, mortgage rates have declined recently, and President Trump seems focused on making housing more affordable. But affordability isn't the only issue; many current homeowners don't want to sell because they locked in mortgage rates of around 3% during the pandemic years. This is causing a lack of turnover in housing. When homes change hands, new owners are more likely to replace appliances, which would greatly benefit Whirlpool. With midterm elections approaching, we might see President Trump take bigger steps to reinvigorate the housing market. We all know that real estate is cyclical, and another housing market supercycle will likely occur at some point. When that happens, Whirlpool could see substantial gains.

4) The Dividend: Whirlpool cut the quarterly dividend in 2025 from $1.75 per share to $0.90 per share. This significantly reduced dividend payout eases pressure on Whirlpool's cash flow. However, this is still very compelling for income investors because it totals $3.60 per share annually, generating a yield of about 6%. With many money market funds now yielding less than 4%, and with the Federal Reserve expected to ease interest rates further into 2026, Whirlpool shares appear undervalued. Of course, income investors typically want to see share price stability, and we have not seen that with Whirlpool lately, but if the stock does stabilize here and management doesn't unload more surprises, this stock could rebound based on the dividend.

5) Costs Reduction Plans And Improved Balance Sheet Strength: Whirlpool achieved a $200 million cost reduction program in 2025 and set a goal to reduce costs by $150 million in 2026. The company is moving more component manufacturing in-house in order to reduce reliance on third-party suppliers and to mitigate tariff headwinds. It is also increasing the use of robotics to offset rising labor expenses, and it has announced layoffs. In March 2026, the company announced 341 layoffs at its manufacturing plant in Iowa. While the stock has declined over the dividend cut last year and the recent capital raise, there are some positives going forward. The company has more flexibility and more cash flow with a reduced dividend, and the balance sheet is stronger. Plus, interest costs are expected to drop in 2026.

Where I Could Be Wrong

With a dividend cut and a capital raise out of the way, I am less concerned about company-specific issues and more worried about macro risks. I believe the risks of a recession are higher than what the stock market seems to be pricing in. Recent weakness in the job market and increasing AI-related layoffs could get worse. Many consumers are showing signs of being financially challenged, and this could lead to weaker consumer spending in the months ahead. The housing market remains stagnant, and getting mortgage rates to go much lower from here could be challenging. All of these issues could continue to weigh on this stock.

In Summary

While I have less faith in management after the recent capital raise, I also recognize that the past few years have been challenging due to the frozen housing market and tariffs. I also want to recognize that it might be time to consider the dividend cut and the capital raise as a reset, which includes resetting the stock price to around $60 per share now. Whirlpool now has a leaner balance sheet, strong market share, and a domestic manufacturing footprint that serves as a natural hedge against global trade volatility. Whirlpool shares might now be stabilizing and coiled for the potentially eventual housing recovery. The dividend yield of 6% will reward investors while we wait for a broader recovery in the housing market.

data.title: Doubts Emerge About Trump's Marine War Insurance Plan
data.description:
The feasibility and efficacy of President Donald Trump's plan to backstop marine insurers covering shipping in the Persian Gulf is being questioned as the war between the US and Israel and Iran continues.

Trump announced on his Truth Social platform on March 3 that he had ordered the US International Development Finance Corporation (DFC), the international investment arm of the US government, to extend political risk insurance and guarantees "at a very reasonable price" to secure maritime trade on its transit through the Gulf. The president also said the US Navy would begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping lane for seabound energy that has been obstructed since the war began. The DFC has also issued a statement on the plan.

The move follows news that insurers have been restricting coverage and raising prices for some marine war insurance for journeys through the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz in particular since the war started on Feb. 28. Iran has responded to US and Israeli airstrikes with widespread missile and drone attacks across the Gulf region.

"While it's welcome news in theory, and no doubt based in good intentions, there's nothing beyond the post that we've seen and no details as to how this will work in practice," David Smith, head of marine at insurance broker McGill and Partners, said in an emailed comment.

Putting together a plan to protect and insure vessels in the Gulf would be "very difficult," he added. "Until there is any further clarity as to how this will be applied, no one is adapting their plans at this stage."

The DFC said in an email it had no additional details about the initiative.

Unanswered questions

If the DFC plan offers marine war cover at pre-conflict rates, "they would be effectively setting a market cap that private insurers will be forced to acknowledge," which should result in cheaper war insurance prices, Jon Werner, partner at Montgomery McCracken Walker & Rhoads, said in emailed comments.

The DFC has offered war risk reinsurance before, but cargo or direct hull insurance would be a "radical departure," according to Werner. The main concern for insurers and shipowners is how any claims would be handled, Werner said, as private insurers have more claims-paying experience than the DFC.

The market has also yet to see whether the DFC's political risk cover will align with standard war risk wording.

"If there is a gap between what the DFC covers and what a shipowner's excess policy requires, it could create significant legal exposure," Werner said.

Coverage by the DFC could come with conditions like mandatory coordination with the US Navy and adherence to specific transit corridors, Werner said. As a result, while coverage might be cheaper, "the long-term cost may be measured in the loss of operational autonomy for shipping lines that choose to opt-in."

Ship safety is as much of a problem as insurance availability and price when passing through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump's proposal is likely to have "limited success in clearing the current shipping backlog," according to credit rating agency Morningstar DBRS.

"As long as the security situation remains volatile, many shipowners may remain reluctant to transit the strait even if government-backed coverage becomes available," Morningstar DBRS said in a March 5 report.

While still nebulous, Trump's plan is finding some support. The initiative is "extremely important to ships in a war-torn region," according to James Mercante, partner at law firm Gallo Vitucci Klar, who said in an email that stands to keep vessels with jet fuel, vehicles and munitions moving.

Cover still available

Marine war coverage is still available in the private market, and the market continues to function.

"Iran and the Persian Gulf is, of course, currently an area of maximum risk severity, but insurance is still available to operators in the area, including the Strait of Hormuz," Chris Jones, CEO of the International Underwriting Association, a trade body representing non-Lloyd's underwriters in the London market, said in a press statement.

Capacity can be found for marine war risks in the Lloyd's of London insurance market and the wider London market, Angus Blayney, marine divisional director at insurance broker Arthur J. Gallagher & Co., said in emailed comments.

"This availability applies to both vessels that are currently in the Persian Gulf and not planning to exit the region, plus those that are looking to travel, enter, or exit the area via the Strait of Hormuz," Blayney said. Prices are higher than shipowners and charterers are used to, but "marine insurers are very much continuing to provide cover," he added.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Verra Mobility: Mixed Near-Term Story Forces Me To Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Verra Mobility Corp. (VRRM) previously with a buy rating because fundamentals continued to nue came in at $improve. The NYCDOT contract win also removed a key uncertainty in the equity story. New growth levers also make the growth runway much more visible. I no longer think the setup is as straightforward as I thought. Revenue momentum is still good, but the margin outlook has weakened much more than I expected. That shift changes the near-term earnings story and is the main reason I am moving to a neutral view.

4Q25 earnings

VRRM's total revenue came in at $257.9 million, which was a solid 16.4% y/y growth. Commercial Services generated $108.1 million in revenue, up 10% y/y, with RAC tolling revenue being the main driver, which grew 16%. The weaker area here was FMC, where revenue fell 8%. Segment profit saw positive growth of 7%, and margin came in at 64%, lower than Q4 2024 because of modestly higher credit loss expense and one-time costs. Government Solutions was the fastest-growing segment, which saw revenue up 25% y/y. The biggest driver was the New York City red-light expansion. Segment profit, however, only came in at $31 million because margin fell to ~24% (down 1000 bps y/y). This quarter, overall, came in ahead of management’s expectations, but the profit numbers were not that great in my view. Adj. EBITDA was essentially flat despite revenue growth, and adj. EPS was down 9.3% y/y to $0.30.

The reasons that changed my rating to neutral

For what it’s worth, I do believe the revenue story looks better now, but the near-term earnings story looks so much worse. The main anchor to my bull case was the NYCDOT win. The idea was that this would add to the recurring revenue base and should support stronger growth and better profitability over time. I also thought the cost pressure around New York was more about upfront spending than something that would stay. I was entirely wrong on the cost side. To be fair, the positive side is real. Management said the New York City contract was signed and registered at the end of December 2025, and the total contract value now stands at $998 million over five years (basically the entire FY2025 revenue base), with an option for a five-year renewal. Q4 already included about $22 million of revenue tied to New York City red-light installations.

Aside from New York, there were also wins outside this region that made the growth story more durable (not just a one-contract story). VRRM booked ~$23 million of incremental annual recurring revenue in Q4, pushing FY2025 bookings to ~$64 million. Management also pointed to wins in Orlando, Pittsburgh, and Hawaii and said the US addressable market has expanded by ~$365 million from new enabling legislation over the past three years, with the potential to reach ~$500 million if California passes additional statewide speed-enforcement legislation. These developments and data points support the demand side of the thesis.

The problem is the margin side of the story. Management now says Government Solutions margins are expected to be down ~450 to 500 basis points in FY2026, mostly due to service pricing changes in the renewed New York contract and minority- and women-owned business subcontractor requirements. Both of them dealt a big dent to my confidence. For the former, it changed how I think about renewals. New York is VRRM’s flagship customer. If the renewal came with a lower pricing reset, it suggests future renewals may not automatically carry the same economics. Contract size could get larger, but it could come with tighter pricing, which means the quality of earnings growth turns weaker (margins more dependent on volume, which is a variable). And if New York can do this, it is very likely that all other regions will do the same when their renewal time comes. On the latter, it is also a big problem, as management said those subcontractor requirements amount to ~$22 million to $24 million annually beginning in FY2026. This literally changes the contract economics (lower margins). Put together, you have lowered pricing and increased cost, and that does not paint a good image of the margin expansion runway.

Management did lay out a path for margin recovery through MOSAIC (VRRM cloud-based end-to-end automated enforcement platform for the Government Solutions business). The guide was that MOSAIC investment should be roughly cost neutral in 2H2026, because the savings should offset the investment cost, and then starting in 2027, it should deliver $10 million to $20 million of annual operating expense savings vs. the 2026 run rate. The key thing here is timing. It implies that 1H 2026 margins would get a lot worse before they recover.

My experience in such cases where margins compress before savings show up is that the market does not usually give full credit for the future payoff. The market likely just focuses on weaker near-term earnings and less room for execution mistakes. Add on the fact that the Government Solutions segment is also dealing with lower New York service pricing and the subcontractor burden, and it makes the margin outlook even worse. MOSAIC may help later, but in the near term it does not solve the problem. All of these basically mean that 2026 is a transition year and push more of the earnings growth recovery story into 2027.

Valuation

VRRM valuation took a beating for a reason. I really like the long-term setup, but it's hard to fight against the near-term narrative, as margins could get a lot worse before they recover. This is something that the market clearly cares about (stock price fell as much as ~17% post the Q4 earnings). The debate is now whether VRRM can recover margin as management guided, and it is virtually impossible to know the progress until the next results. Until then, I think the stock likely stays range-bound at ~12x NTM earnings since near-term expected earnings growth is low-single-digits. Compare this to the S&P 500 index that has low-to-mid-teens EPS growth and trades at 21x; I’d say the index is more attractive.

Conclusion

I downgrade VRRM to a neutral rating. I still like the long-term setup, and I do not think the demand story is broken. The New York story is real, bookings remain healthy, and there is still growth potential outside the core market. The problem is that the margin reset looks more serious than I expected, with lower pricing, higher subcontractor costs, and MOSAIC savings only coming in FY2027. That makes 2026 a transition year, and I typically don’t like to invest during such periods. Until there is clearer proof that margins are recovering, I do not see a strong reason to turn bullish here.
